from_rulebook_and_forum.D: All attacking knights are always eliminated in a Jihad, regardless of the number of defenders.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: Knights and rooks never move from one empire to another as a result of a religious war. The attackers and defenders are eliminated, and if the attacking card has agent icons, those agents are placed in the empty spaces in Hungary if they survived.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: Rulebook, p. 25, 'Campaign Op Example' Tip; BGG Forum, ASKindred, 2024-11-23T12:39:08-06:00

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you successfully execute a vote or civil war against a Republic, does the Republic card flip back to its Empire (golden) side?
from_scratch.A: Yes - Successful votes or civil wars against a Republic always flip it back to its Empire side.
from_scratch.B: No - Successful votes or civil wars against a Republic have no effect on the card's orientation.
from_scratch.C: Yes, but only if the successful action was a Strawman vote; civil wars have a different effect.
from_scratch.D: No - Republics can only be flipped to Empires through specific event cards, not votes or civil wars.
from_scratch.Explanation: The rules state: E4.e (Strawman vote victory flips the card), F6.c (If the card is in your Tableau, you flip it), and J.d (Civil war victory flips the card if the Empire Square is in your Tableau). These all indicate a flip to the other side (King/Republic, which translates to Empire/Republic) upon a successful action of the specified types.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you perform a successful vote or civil war on a republic, does it revert to the golden side and become an empire?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – a successful vote or civil war against a Republic flips the Empire Square to its King side.
from_rulebook.B: No – a Republic always remains a Republic, regardless of votes or civil wars.
from_rulebook.C: Yes – but only if the Republic is in an opponent's Tableau, not your own.
from_rulebook.D: No – Republics can only be changed back to Kings via a coronation.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The rules state that a victorious Strawman vote against your own King creates a Republic and vice versa. Also, if victorious in a civil war, and the Empire Square is in your Tableau, you flip it to its other side (King or Republic).

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you perform a successful vote or civil war on a republic, does it revert to its golden side and become an empire?
from_forum.A: Yes, a successful vote or civil war on a republic will flip the Empire Square in your tableau to its golden (Empire) side.
from_forum.B: No, a republic remains a republic even after a successful vote or civil war; only empires can change.
from_forum.C: Yes, but only if the civil war is against an opponent; a vote never changes the regime type.
from_forum.D: No, the regime type (Empire or Republic) is fixed once established and cannot be changed by votes or civil wars.
from_forum.Explanation: The rules state that a successful Strawman vote against your own King creates a Republic, and vice versa.  Similarly, a successful civil war where the Empire Square is in your Tableau results in it being flipped to its other side.  This means a Republic would flip to an Empire.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you perform a successful vote or civil war on a republic, does it revert to its golden side and become an empire?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes – if you perform a successful vote or civil war on a republic in your Tableau, it flips back to its King side.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: No – a republic, once established, cannot be changed back into a kingdom.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: Yes, but only if the republic has no repressed tokens.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: No – republics can only be changed via a successful campaign action from another King.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The rules state that a successful vote or civil war on an Empire Square *in your Tableau* flips it to its other side. This applies whether it's currently a King (becoming a Republic) or a Republic (becoming a King).

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when can you place rooks on a knight symbol and knights on a rook symbol?
from_scratch.A: You can only place rooks on rook symbols and knights on knight symbols, regardless of the action being taken.
from_scratch.B: You can place any agent on any symbol only when levying agents through trade fairs or tax actions.
from_scratch.C: You can place any agent on any symbol when you play a card. The symbols are only relevant for trade fairs and tax actions.
from_scratch.D: You can never place rooks on knight symbols or knights on rook symbols; agents must always match the symbol they are placed on.
from_scratch.Explanation: The rules of Pax Renaissance (2nd Ed.) state that agent placement during card play ignores the symbol. Symbols are only used when levying agents through trade fairs or tax actions.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition, when can you place rooks on a knight symbol and knights on a rook symbol?
from_rulebook.A: You can never place rooks on knight symbols or knights on rook symbols.
from_rulebook.B: You can place rooks on knight symbols and knights on rook symbols only when levying agents through trade fairs or tax actions.
from_rulebook.C: You can place any agent on any symbol when you play a card, regardless of the symbol.
from_rulebook.D: You can place rooks on knight symbols and knights on rook symbols only during a Regime Change.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The rules state that when playing a card, you may place the Agents depicted according to the Location listed. The symbols (Knight or Rook) within the cities are only relevant for Levies. When placing agents due to card play, you can ignore the symbol.
from_rulebook.Citation: BOOK I - RULES, E2. PLAY HAND CARD & PLACE AGENTS; B6.a; F4; G4

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when can you place rooks on a knight symbol and knights on a rook symbol?
from_forum.A: You can place any agent on any symbol when you play a card. The symbols only matter when levying agents through trade fairs or tax actions.
from_forum.B: You can only place rooks on knight symbols and knights on rook symbols after a successful regime change.
from_forum.C: You can never place rooks on knight symbols or knights on rook symbols; the pieces must match the printed symbol.
from_forum.D: You can place any agent on any symbol only during the 'Emancipation' phase after a regime change, provided there is an empty space.
from_forum.Explanation: The game allows players to place any agent (knight or rook) on any symbol (knight or rook) when playing a card. The printed symbols on the map are only relevant during the levying of agents via trade fairs or tax actions. Also, after a successful regime change, you can move any repressed knight or rook to an empty city within that empire, or pay 1 florin to repress whatever is already there. In this case, the printed symbol does not matter either. After a battle, you can repress for free if all cities are full.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when can you place rooks on a knight symbol and knights on a rook symbol?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: You can place any agent on any symbol when you play a card. The symbols are only used when levying agents through trade fairs or tax actions.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: You can only place rooks on knight symbols and knights on rook symbols when levying agents through trade fairs.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: You can never place rooks on knight symbols or knights on rook symbols; the symbols must always match.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: You can place rooks on knight symbols and knights on rook symbols after a successful regime change, regardless of whether there was a battle or not.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The type of agent (rook or knight) being placed is independent of the city icon when placing agents as a result of playing a card. The city icons (rook or knight symbols) restrict placement only during levying via trade fairs or tax actions.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: BGG forum discussion: username: Kevin Lee Thatcher, post_date: 2024-03-22T15:08:38-05:00; Rulebook, E2.2b

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the Circassian Mamluks' Mamluk Golden Age Ability and its associated erratum, can the 'virtual' bishops created by this ability be used by *other* players to achieve victory?
from_scratch.A: No, the 'virtual' bishops are exclusively for the use of the player controlling the Circassian Mamluks, contributing only to their own victory conditions.
from_scratch.B: Yes, if Islam becomes the supreme religion due to these 'virtual' bishops, and another player has more Islamic Prestige than the Mamluk player, that other player can win.
from_scratch.C: No, 'virtual' pieces, according to a general rule across all Pax games, can never contribute to another player's victory.
from_scratch.D: Yes, but only if the other player also controls a card with the Mamluk Golden Age Ability, allowing them to 'share' the virtual bishops.
from_scratch.Explanation: The erratum clarifies that the 'virtual' bishops contribute to determining the supreme religion. If Islam becomes supreme because of them, any player (not just the Mamluk player) with the most Islamic Prestige can win. The virtual bishops can also prevent other players from winning if they would have otherwise had a supreme religion.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the erratum for the Circassian Mamluks' Mamluk Golden Age Ability mean that someone else can use these 'virtual' bishops to win?
from_rulebook.A: No, 'virtual' bishops are only considered for the player who owns the Circassian Mamluks card.
from_rulebook.B: Yes, 'virtual' bishops count towards the total number of bishops of a religion in play, potentially helping any player achieve a Holy Victory.
from_rulebook.C: No, the Mamluk Golden Age ability only affects trade routes, not victory conditions.
from_rulebook.D: Yes, but only if the player attempting to win controls the Mamluk Empire.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The provided answer states that 'virtual' bishops can help other players win if it makes Islam the supreme religion and they have more Islamic Prestige. This implies the 'virtual' bishops count towards the total for determining religious supremacy, which is a condition for Holy Victory.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the Circassian Mamluks' Mamluk Golden Age Ability erratum, can another player use the 'virtual' bishops created by this ability to achieve a Holy Victory?
from_forum.A: No, Prestige can only be used by the player who owns it for claiming victory.
from_forum.B: Yes, the 'virtual' bishops are always active when determining the Supreme Religion, potentially aiding or hindering any player's Holy Victory attempt.
from_forum.C: The 'virtual' bishops only apply to the player who owns the Circassian Mamluks card.
from_forum.D: The number of 'virtual' bishops is equal to *your* Patron Prestige, regardless of who owns the Circassian Mamluks.

This affects the determination of the Supreme Religion for *all* players, potentially helping another player win if Islam becomes supreme and they have the most Islamic Prestige, or preventing a win if another religion would have otherwise been supreme.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), the erratum for the Circassian Mamluks' Mamluk Golden Age Ability states: "Your [Patron Prestige] count as green Bishops in a Holy victory." Does this mean another player can use these 'virtual' bishops to win?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: No, Prestige can only be used by the player who owns it for claiming victory.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Yes, but only if the player with the Circassian Mamluks card is eliminated from the game.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: No, the 'virtual' bishops only affect the count for determining the Supreme Religion, not the Prestige count of individual players.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: Yes, any player can freely use any other player's Prestige and Abilities to win
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The 'virtual' bishops contribute to the total count of Bishops for determining the Supreme Religion (Islam, in this case). This can help any player attempting a Holy Victory if Islam becomes supreme due to these 'virtual' bishops, or hinder other players if it prevents another religion from being supreme. The ability affects the overall game state, not just the owning player's victory condition.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), according to rule E2.1a, when is a Bishop placed relative to a regime change during a battle?
from_scratch.A: The Bishop is placed before any regime change occurs, as the Bishop's placement is considered a separate action from the battle itself.
from_scratch.B: The Bishop is placed simultaneously with the regime change; both actions occur at the same time during battle resolution.
from_scratch.C: The Bishop is placed after the regime change, as regime change is part of the battle resolution and occurs before Bishop placement.
from_scratch.D: The Bishop is placed only if the regime change is unsuccessful; otherwise, no Bishop is placed.
from_scratch.Explanation: The Bishop is placed after the battle, which includes regime change. According to the order of operations in the rulebook, regime change is part of the battle resolution and occurs before the Bishop is placed. This is clarified in the Battle glossary entry, which outlines that regime change is the final part of the battle before placing the Bishop.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), according to rule E2.1a, when is a Bishop placed in relation to a battle that results in a regime change?
from_rulebook.A: The Bishop is placed before the battle, and participates as an attacker.
from_rulebook.B: The Bishop is placed before the battle, but does not participate in the fighting.
from_rulebook.C: The Bishop is placed after the battle, which includes regime change.
from_rulebook.D: The Bishop is placed during the battle, after casualties are determined but before regime change.
from_rulebook.Explanation: According to the order of operations, regime change is part of the battle resolution and occurs before the Bishop is placed. Bishop Agents do not participate in the Battle but must be placed after the Battle per E2.2a.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), according to rule E2.1a, when is a Bishop placed in relation to regime change during a battle?
from_forum.A: The Bishop is placed before regime change, as Bishops do not participate in the battle itself.
from_forum.B: The Bishop is placed before regime change, allowing the player to potentially eliminate the Bishop by placing it on the 'conquered' empire.
from_forum.C: The Bishop is placed simultaneously with regime change, as both are considered part of the battle resolution.
from_forum.D: The Bishop is placed after regime change, as regime change is the final part of the battle.
from_forum.Explanation: Regime change is part of the battle resolution and occurs before the Bishop is placed. The Battle glossary entry clarifies that regime change is the final part of the battle before placing the Bishop.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if a card allows you to place 2 bishops and you place them both on the *same* card, does this trigger the Diet of Worms, resulting in both bishops being killed?
from_scratch.A: Yes, placing both bishops on the same card always triggers the Diet of Worms, and both bishops are killed.
from_scratch.B: No, the Diet of Worms is only triggered if you place bishops on different cards.
from_scratch.C: Yes, but only if the card played also has a one-shot effect that is triggered; otherwise, placing the bishops is optional.
from_scratch.D: No, the Diet of Worms is a separate action that must be taken independently of placing bishops.
from_scratch.Explanation: Placing both bishops on a single card activates the Diet of Worms, resulting in both bishops being killed. While agent placement is generally optional, the question specifically asks about the scenario where *both* are placed on the *same* card, which *always* triggers the Diet of Worms. The one-shot is only relevant to whether placement is mandatory, not whether Diet of Worms is triggered *if* you place them on the same card.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you play a card that allows you to place 2 Bishops and you decide to place them on a single card, does this activate the Diet of Worms and result in both Bishops being killed?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – placing both Bishops on a single card activates the Diet of Worms, resulting in both Bishops being killed.
from_rulebook.B: No – the Diet of Worms is only activated when placing Bishops using an Inquisitor Op, not when placing them as Agents during card play.
from_rulebook.C: No – you can only place one Bishop on a card at a time. The second Bishop must be placed on a different card.
from_rulebook.D: Yes – but only if the card you are playing has the 'Diet of Worms' Ability listed on it.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The Diet of Worms rule states that if a Bishop enters a card containing another Bishop, both are Killed. Placing two Bishops as Agents counts as each Bishop 'entering' the card.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section F1. INQUISITOR (RELIGIOUS OP), a. Diet Of Worms Rule.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you have a Mamluk card with a 'behead' operation, can you use it to behead an EAST card that lacks a specific region (e.g., 'Ottoman', 'Mamluk', etc.)?
from_scratch.A: Yes - EAST cards without a specific region are considered part of all Eastern empires and are valid targets.
from_scratch.B: No - 'Behead' operations can only target cards with a specific named region matching the attacker's region or affiliation.
from_scratch.C: No -  EAST cards without a specific region are considered 'stateless' and immune to targeted operations like 'behead'.
from_scratch.D: Yes - but only if you also control another card of the same suit (e.g., another King) as the target card.
from_scratch.Explanation: Cards representing EAST without a specific region are considered to belong to *all* Eastern empires simultaneously.  This makes them vulnerable to any 'behead' action originating from an EAST card, regardless of specific empire affiliation.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition, if you have a Mamluk card with a behead operation, can you behead an EAST card without a specific region?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, you can behead an EAST card without a specific region. These cards are considered part of all Eastern empires simultaneously, making them vulnerable to behead actions from any EAST card.
from_rulebook.B: No, you can only behead cards that specifically state 'Mamluk' as their Location.
from_rulebook.C: No, the behead operation can only target cards within the same Tableau (East or West) as the card with the behead operation, regardless of Location.
from_rulebook.D: Yes, but only if the EAST card without a specific region is in your own Tableau.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Cards with a Location of "The East" are considered to be in all of the Eastern Locations (Hungary, Ottoman, Byzantium, Mamluk). The behead operation can target a card if the acting card's Location matches the target's Location. Since a Mamluk card can target "The East" and the target card is considered to be in all Eastern Locations, the behead is legal.
from_rulebook.Citation: Rulebook, Section F3, E4d, LOCATION (B5b, and Glossary)

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you have a Mamluk card with a behead operation, can you behead an EAST card that does not have a specific region listed?

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding Campaigns and Religious Wars, how do Knights from adjacent Thrones participate in attacks, and is the glossary entry about adjacent Knights in Campaigns a typo?
from_scratch.A: Knights from adjacent Thrones always join the attack in both Campaigns and Religious Wars, regardless of vassalage.
from_scratch.B: Knights from adjacent Thrones join the attack in Campaigns only if they are vassals of the campaigning King; they always join in Religious Wars.
from_scratch.C: Knights from adjacent Thrones *only* join in Religious Wars, not campaigns. In campaigns only knights from the attacking empire participate. The glossary entry clarifies that knights can *fight in* adjacent locations in both, but the *source* of attackers differs.
from_scratch.D: Knights from adjacent Thrones never join the attack in either Campaigns or Religious Wars. The glossary entry about adjacent Knights is a typo and should only refer to Religious Wars.
from_scratch.Explanation: The Knights participating in the Campaign are only from the attacking empire, and the campaigning empire can only target an adjacent empire. The glossary entry emphasizes that knights can fight adjacent locations only in campaigns and religious wars, but the attackers on these occasions are not determined by the same rules.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), do knights from adjacent thrones join the attack in a campaign as they do in religious wars? If they are vassals of the king campaigning, do they join? Is the glossary entry about adjacent knights attacking in a campaign a typo?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, knights from adjacent thrones join the attack, regardless of vassal status. The glossary entry is correct.
from_rulebook.B: No, only knights from the campaigning empire's location join the attack. Vassal knights do not join. The glossary entry is a typo.
from_rulebook.C: Knights from adjacent thrones only join if they are vassals of the campaigning king. The glossary entry is correct.
from_rulebook.D: No, only knights from the attacking empire's location join the attack, unless they are vassals of the campaigning king, in which case they also join. The glossary entry clarifies this distinction.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Knights participating in a Campaign are only from the attacking empire's location. Vassals do not join the campaign. The glossary entry clarifies that knights can fight in adjacent locations in both campaigns and religious wars, but the *source* of those attacking knights is different. In a Religious war, adjacent knights of the correct religion join. In a campaign, only knights *in the attacking King's location* attack.
from_rulebook.Citation: F9. Campaign, K1. Crusade, Reformation, or Jihad One-Shot

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding Campaigns, do Knights from adjacent thrones join the attack like in Religious Wars? Do vassal Knights of the campaigning King join? Is the glossary entry about adjacent Knights attacking in campaigns a typo?
from_forum.A: Yes, Knights from adjacent thrones join the attack in Campaigns, similar to Religious Wars, and vassal Knights also join.
from_forum.B: The glossary entry is a typo; Knights do not participate in Campaigns at all.

from_forum.D: Knights from adjacent thrones only join the Campaign if they are vassals of the campaigning King.
from_forum.Explanation: The Knights participating in the Campaign are only from the attacking empire, and the campaigning empire can only target an adjacent empire. The glossary entry emphasizes that knights can fight adjacent locations only in campaigns and religious wars, but the attackers on these occasions are not determined by the same rules.

from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes, knights from adjacent thrones join the attack in a campaign, just as they do in religious wars. Vassal knights of the campaigning king also join.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), a player has Aragon cards in their East tableau (due to being purchased from the East market).  They want to use a bishop on an Aragon card to jump to a card located in 'The East' in *another* player's tableau.  The game manual defines 'The East' as the four Eastern kingdoms, *excluding* Aragon.  Was it correct to rule that the bishop *could not* make this jump?
from_scratch.A: No, the bishop can jump.  Since the Aragon card is in the East tableau, it counts as being in 'The East' for all game effects.
from_scratch.B: Yes, the bishop can only jump to cards whose location icon matches one of the four kingdoms defined as 'The East'. Aragon's location icon is not one of those four.
from_scratch.C: No, the bishop can jump. Bishops can always jump between tableaux, regardless of location, as long as the target card is in 'The East'.
from_scratch.D: Yes, the bishop can only jump if both players agree, since the rules are ambiguous regarding tableau location versus card location.
from_scratch.Explanation: The ruling was correct.  The bishop's movement is restricted by the *location icon* on the cards, not the tableau they reside in.  The East tableau indicates where a card *operates*, not its inherent location.  Aragon is explicitly excluded from the definition of 'The East', so a bishop on an Aragon card cannot jump to a card located in 'The East'.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), a player had Aragon cards in their East tableau. They argued that a bishop located on such an Aragon card should be able to jump to a card located in 'The East' in another player's tableau. Was it correct to rule that the bishop couldn't make the jump?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – Bishops can only move to cards with a printed Location matching their current card's Location, or to Adjacent cards in the same Tableau. 'The East' is not a specific Location, but a region.
from_rulebook.B: No – Because the Aragon card is in the East tableau, it is considered to be in 'The East', allowing the Bishop to move to any card in that region.
from_rulebook.C: No – Bishops can move freely between Tableaux as long as the destination card's Location is in the same region (East or West) as the originating card's Tableau.
from_rulebook.D: Yes – Bishops can move from East to West if cards share the same Location.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The Bishop cannot move from a card with the Aragon location to a card with 'The East' as its location because Aragon is not located in the East. The sides of the tableau do not demarcate location; only the location icons on the cards do. A bishop can move to any Tableau card (even in an opponent’s Tableau) or Throne that has the same Location as the Tableau card being played. 'The East' refers to the four kingdoms in the Eastern part of the map. A bishop can move to a card with 'The East' as a location from another card with 'The East' as a location.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), a player has Aragon cards in their East tableau. A bishop is on an Aragon card. Can this bishop jump to a card located in 'The East' in another player's tableau?
from_forum.A: Yes, because the Aragon card is in the East tableau, the bishop can jump to any card in 'The East'.
from_forum.B: Yes, the bishop can move to any adjacent card, regardless of location, and can therefore jump to a card in 'The East'.
from_forum.C: No, because 'The East' is defined as the four kingdoms in the Eastern part of the map, which excludes Aragon. The tableau side doesn't determine location.
from_forum.D: Yes, a card in the East tableau can be considered to be in any one of the four Eastern Locations for movement purposes.
from_forum.Explanation: The bishop cannot move from a card with the Aragon location to a card with 'The East' as its location because Aragon is not located in the East. The sides of the tableau do not demarcate location; only the location icons on the cards do.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), a player has Aragon cards in their East tableau. Can a Bishop located on an Aragon card in the East tableau jump to a card located in 'The East' in another player's tableau, assuming the cards are not adjacent?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes, because any card in the East tableau is considered to be located in 'The East'.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Yes, because Bishops can move freely between tableaus, regardless of location.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: No, because 'The East' is defined as the four kingdoms in the Eastern part of the map, which does not include Aragon.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: No, because a Bishop can only move to adjacent cards, and the question specifies they are not adjacent.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The Bishop cannot move from a card with the Aragon location to a card with 'The East' as its location because Aragon is not located in the East. The sides of the tableau do not demarcate location; only the location icons on the cards do. The tableau side only determines which Ops are activated.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), how many coins will a player receive for *one* concession when launching a Western trade fair from the Moluccas if they have *both* the Michelangelo card and the Indian Armada card in their bank, which each state that concessions count twice?
from_scratch.A: 1 florin
from_scratch.B: 2 florins
from_scratch.C: 4 florins
from_scratch.D: 8 florins
from_scratch.Explanation: According to the FAQ, the abilities of Michelangelo and Indian Armada are cumulative. Each concession normally yields 1 florin.  Michelangelo doubles this (x2), and Indian Armada doubles it again (x2), resulting in a total multiplier of 4x (2 x 2 = 4). Therefore, one concession yields 4 florins.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), how many coins will a player get for one concession when launching a Western trade fair from the Moluccas if they have two cards laid out in the bank that allow concessions to count twice (Michelangelo card, Indian Armada card)?
from_rulebook.A: 1 florin
from_rulebook.B: 2 florins
from_rulebook.C: 4 florins
from_rulebook.D: 8 florins
from_rulebook.Explanation: Each concession would yield 4 florins when launching a Western trade fair from the Moluccas with two cards that allow concessions to count twice. This is because the abilities are cumulative, and each concession counts as 2x for each card, resulting in a total multiplier of 4x.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), how many coins will a player get for one concession when launching a Western trade fair from the Moluccas if they have two cards laid out in the bank that allow concessions to count twice (Michelangelo card, Indian Armada card)?

from_forum.Explanation: Each concession would yield 4 florins. The abilities are cumulative, and each concession counts as 2x for each card, resulting in a total multiplier of 4x.  A single concession normally yields 1 coin, and 1 * 2 * 2 = 4.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the 'E2. PLAY HARD CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle' rule:  If all available cities and borders are already occupied by agents, and you have no florins, can you still use this 'One-Shot' action, and are you *always* required to attempt to place agents and suppress existing tokens?
from_scratch.A: No, you cannot use the 'One-Shot' action if you have no florins.  Agent placement and suppression (paying 1 florin to China) are mandatory parts of the action, even if all locations are occupied.
from_scratch.B: Yes, you can still use the 'One-Shot' action, but you skip the agent placement and suppression steps since you have no florins and all locations are occupied.
from_scratch.C: You can use the 'One-Shot' action, but you only skip the suppression (paying 1 florin to China) because you have no florins. You must still *attempt* to place agents, even if all locations are occupied.
from_scratch.D: You can use the 'One-Shot' action, but you are not required to *attempt* agent placement if all locations are occupied. The florin payment to China is only required if you successfully place an agent.
from_scratch.Explanation: The rules for 'E2. PLAY HARD CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle' require mandatory agent placement (even if all locations are occupied, you still go through the motions) and mandatory suppression, which costs 1 florin paid to China.  If you cannot pay the florin, you cannot perform the 'No Battle' One-Shot action at all.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the 'E2. PLAY HAND CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle' rule, are you required to place agents even if all cities or borders are occupied, must you pay 1 florin to China to suppress existing tokens, and can you use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action if you don't have florins?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, you must place agents, and suppression is mandatory, costing 1 florin to China. You cannot use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action if you cannot pay.
from_rulebook.B: No, you are not required to place agents if all locations are occupied. Suppression is optional, and you can still use the action without florins.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, you must place agents, but suppression is optional. You can use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action even without florins.
from_rulebook.D: No, you are not required to place agents, and suppression is not applicable. The action can be used regardless of florin availability.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The rules state that if a One-Shot occurs with no battle, the Agent(s) *must* be deployed (E2.1.b).  If placing an Agent on an occupied City or Border, you *must* Repress the existing Token (E2.2.b, E2.2.d), and this requires paying 1 Florin to China for each Token repressed. If you cannot pay the Florin, you cannot perform the required action of repressing, and therefore you cannot use the One-shot.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), under rule E2. 'PLAY HARD CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle', if all cities or borders are occupied, must I still place agents, and pay 1 florin to China to suppress existing tokens? What if I don't have any florins?
from_forum.A: Yes, you must place agents and suppress existing ones by paying 1 florin to China. If you have no florins, you cannot use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action.
from_forum.B: No, you only place agents if there is space available. You are not required to suppress existing tokens.
from_forum.C: Yes, agent placement is mandatory, but suppression and payment are only required if there's an open space.
from_forum.D: You can choose to place agents or not. If you place agents and there's no space, you must suppress, but if you have no florins, you can still place the agents without suppressing.
from_forum.Explanation: Agent placement is mandatory for 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' actions. If all spaces are occupied, you must suppress existing agents, which requires paying 1 florin to China. If you cannot pay, you cannot perform the action.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the 'E2. PLAY HAND CARD & PLACE AGENTS, 1. If One-Shot Occurs. b. No Battle' rule, are you required to place agents even if all cities or borders are occupied, must you pay 1 florin to China to suppress existing tokens, and if you lack florins, can you still use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes, you must place agents, and suppression is mandatory, costing 1 florin to China. If you cannot pay, you cannot use the 'One-Shot Occurs. No Battle' action.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: No, you are only required to place agents if there are available spaces. You do not need to suppress existing tokens.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: Yes, you must place agents and suppress existing tokens, but you do not need to pay 1 florin to China.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: No, agent placement is optional in the 'No Battle' scenario, regardless of available space or florins.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The rules and confirmed by Phil Eklund, state that agent placement is mandatory when a One-Shot occurs, even in the 'No Battle' scenario. If all spaces are occupied, you must suppress existing tokens, which costs 1 florin per token paid to China. If you cannot pay the required florins, you cannot use the One-Shot action.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you successfully execute a Vote operation to instigate a Regime Change on a Kingdom in another player's tableau, where does the affected Empire Square end up, and on which side?
from_scratch.A: The Empire Square remains in the original player's tableau, but flips to its Republic side.
from_scratch.B: The Empire Square moves to your tableau, placed on its Republic side.
from_scratch.C: The Empire Square moves to your tableau, placed on its King side, in the furthest East or West position as appropriate.
from_scratch.D: The Empire Square is removed from the game entirely, as the Kingdom has been overthrown.
from_scratch.Explanation: According to the Regime Change rules (Glossary and F6c): 'Unless it is already in your Tableau, move the Empire Square to your Tableau on its King side, in the furthest East or West position as appropriate.'  F6c further clarifies that only Empires already in *your* tableau flip; otherwise, they are gained as Kings.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if you successfully execute a vote operation to change a kingdom in another player's tableau into a Republic, where does the new Republic go?
from_rulebook.A: The target empire becomes a Republic in its current tableau.
from_rulebook.B: The target empire goes into your tableau on its King side.
from_rulebook.C: The target empire goes into your tableau on its Republic side.
from_rulebook.D: The target empire is removed from the game.
from_rulebook.Explanation: A successful regime change initiated by you moves the Empire Square to your Tableau on its King side, regardless of the target empire's previous state. F6c specifies that if the Empire Square is in an opponent's Tableau, you gain it as a King.
from_rulebook.Citation: F6c, Glossary entry for regime change

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), at the end of a player's turn, there are two empty spaces in the market.  Only one deck and one card remain to refill the market. After refilling, one market space remains empty. Does this situation trigger a Patron Victory?
from_scratch.A: No, because the market attempted to refresh, even if it couldn't completely fill.
from_scratch.B: Yes, because the market did not fully refresh, leaving an empty space, and the draw deck is exhausted.
from_scratch.C: No, a Patron Victory can only be triggered at the beginning of a player's turn, not the end.
from_scratch.D: Yes, but only if the active player has more than five victory points.
from_scratch.Explanation: According to rule L2a, a Patron Victory is triggered when the market cannot be fully refreshed because the cards have run out.  The market technically did not refresh because there is still a gap after the one card has been used, and so the market is still in need of refreshment. Since the cards have run out, this triggers a Patron Victory.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition, if at the end of a player's turn there is only one card remaining in the draw decks to fill two empty spaces in the market, and it only fills one of the two spaces, does this activate a patron victory?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, because the market did not fully refresh, a Patron Victory is triggered.
from_rulebook.B: No, because the market technically refreshed, even if only partially, a Patron Victory is not triggered.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, because one of the draw decks is empty, a Patron Victory is triggered.
from_rulebook.D: No, a Patron Victory can only be triggered if both draw decks are completely empty and no cards can be drawn at all.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The market technically did not refresh because there is still a gap after the one card has been used, and so the market is still in need of refreshment. Since the cards have run out, this triggers a Patron Victory by rule L2a.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), at the end of a player's turn, there was one deck and one card remaining to fill two spaces in the market. Although the market technically did refresh, it only filled one of the two spaces. Does this activate a patron victory?
from_forum.A: No, because if one deck runs out, you use the other one to fill spots on the "wrong" side, continuing the game.
from_forum.B: No, because if there is one card left and one gap, the market refreshes, and the game continues. A patron victory only occurs if there are two gaps and only one card to fill the first.
from_forum.C: Yes, because the market attempted to refresh but failed to fill all gaps. The game ends, and victory goes to the player with the most patrons.
from_forum.D: Yes, the market did technically refresh, so it does not activate a patron victory.
from_forum.Explanation: The market technically did not refresh because there is still a gap after the one card has been used. Because the cards have run out, this triggers a Patron Victory.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), at the end of a player's turn, there was one deck and one card remaining to fill two spaces in the market. Although the market technically did refresh, it only filled one of two spaces. Does this activate a patron victory?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: No, the market fully refreshed because all available cards were used.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Yes, the market did not fully refresh, as a gap remains, triggering a Patron Victory.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: No, as long as one deck has cards, the market can always fully refresh.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: Yes, but only if the remaining card is in the East deck.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The market is considered to have not refreshed if gaps remain after drawing all available cards. Since one gap remains and there are no more cards to draw, a Patron Victory is triggered.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), two scenarios arise: 1) An apostasy removes an empire from another player's tableau (as a suzerain). What happens to its vassals? 2) When playing a card like Spice Islands (trade shift one-shot requiring discovery prestige), can the one-shot be triggered using the card's *own* prestige?
from_scratch.A: 1) Vassals remain in play, becoming independent. 2) Yes, the card's own prestige can be used.
from_scratch.B: 1) Vassals are returned to their thrones. 2) No, the prestige must come from another card already in the tableau.
from_scratch.C: 1) Vassals are discarded. 2) Yes, the card's own prestige can be used.
from_scratch.D: 1) Vassals remain in play, becoming suzerains of the attacking player. 2) No, the prestige must be present before the card is even purchased.
from_scratch.Explanation: 1) According to the rules, when a suzerain is removed via apostasy, any vassals are returned to their thrones. 2) The rules specify that for one-shots requiring prestige, you must already possess the required prestige on another card *before* playing the card with the one-shot; the card being played cannot contribute its own prestige to trigger its own one-shot.

from_rulebook.Question: Regarding the Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition) board game, answer the following: 1. If an apostasy removes an empire in another player's tableau as a suzerain, what happens to its vassal? 2. When purchasing a card with a trade shift one-shot (Spice Islands) that requires a discovery prestige, can the one-shot be triggered using the card's own prestige, or must it be from another card already in the tableau?
from_rulebook.A: 1. The vassal remains in the other player's tableau. 2. The card's own prestige can be used.
from_rulebook.B: 1. The vassal is returned to its throne. 2. You must have the required prestige on another card, not counting the card being played.
from_rulebook.C: 1. The vassal becomes a free empire in the player's tableau who controlled the suzerain. 2. The card's own prestige can be used, but only if the card is played to the tableau first.
from_rulebook.D: 1. The vassal is discarded from the game. 2. You can use the prestige of any card in your hand or tableau, including the card being played.
from_rulebook.Explanation: 1. Apostasy causes the discarding of cards. If an Empire Square is discarded, it is returned to its Throne, along with any Vassals. 2. The Spice Islands trade shift one-shot specifically states that the discovery prestige must be from a card already in your Tableau, not the card being played.

from_forum.Question: This question concerns the rules of Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition).  Consider two scenarios: (1) An apostasy removes a suzerain empire from another player's tableau. What happens to the suzerain's vassals? (2) A player purchases the Spice Islands card, which has a trade shift one-shot requiring discovery prestige, and the card itself has discovery prestige. from_forum.A: (1) Vassals remain where they are, as only the suzerain is affected. (2) Yes, the prestige on the Spice Islands card itself can be used to trigger the one-shot.
from_forum.B: (1) Vassals are returned to their thrones. (2) No, the prestige must be from another card already in the player's tableau.
from_forum.C: (1) Vassals remain where they are, because Apostasy does not trigger Regime Change. (2) No, the prestige must be from another card already in the player's tableau.
from_forum.D: (1) Vassals are returned to their thrones. (2) Yes, because playing the card adds its prestige to the tableau, allowing the one-shot.
from_forum.Explanation: (1) When an empire is discarded (as with Apostasy), any vassals are also returned to their thrones. (2) The Spice Islands one-shot specifically requires prestige from another card in the tableau, not the Spice Islands card itself.

from_scratch.C: The Bishop is removed from the game, unless the beheaded card is a King, in which case the Bishop returns to the Throne.
from_scratch.D: The Bishop remains on the card, unaffected by the beheading action.
from_scratch.Explanation: When a bishop is on a beheaded card, it is removed from the game. However, there is an exception: when a king is beheaded, the bishop follows it back to the throne.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a bishop is on a beheaded card, is it removed from the game?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, bishops on beheaded cards are always removed from the game.
from_rulebook.B: No, bishops on beheaded cards are moved to an adjacent card in the same tableau.
from_rulebook.C: Yes, unless the beheaded card is a king, in which case the bishop moves to the throne.
from_rulebook.D: No, the bishop remains on the card and is discarded with it.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Cards (and any Bishops on them) are discarded as a result of selling cards (E3), beheading (F3), apostasy (K3), trade fairs (G1b), or Comet purchase (L1). Remove discarded cards from the game, and return discarded Tokens to the general pool or player Reserves.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a bishop is on a beheaded card, is it removed from the game?
from_forum.A: Yes, the bishop is always removed from the game when on a beheaded card.
from_forum.B: Yes, the bishop is removed from the game when on a beheaded card, unless a king is beheaded; then the bishop returns to the throne.
from_forum.C: No, the bishop is never removed from the game; it is simply moved to another location.
from_forum.D: Yes, the bishop is removed from the game and the card is sold by its owner.
from_forum.Explanation: Bishops on beheaded cards are removed from the game, with one exception: if a king is beheaded, any bishop on that card returns to the throne with the king.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a bishop is on a beheaded card, is it removed from the game?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes, the bishop is always removed from the game when the card it is on is beheaded.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: No, the bishop is never removed from the game; it always moves to another card.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: Yes, the bishop is removed from the game, unless the beheaded card is a king, in which case the bishop returns to the throne.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: No, the bishop is moved to the supply, available for placement again.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: Bishops on beheaded cards are removed from the game. However, if a king is beheaded, any bishop on that card returns to the throne with the king. Bishops are not placed in supply and are not available in general.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), Card A has the ability 'May do east & west ops in one action' and Card B has the ability 'you may activate eastern ops without expending an action'. Can these abilities be combined to activate eastern ops without an action and then perform west ops using Card A's ability?
from_scratch.A: Yes, Card B's free eastern op allows Card A's ability to then trigger, letting you perform a west op.
from_scratch.B: Yes, these abilities can always be combined as long as you have both cards.
from_scratch.C: No, because you cannot perform east ops twice in the same turn, using Card B's ability prevents you from using the east op component of Card A's ability.
from_scratch.D: No, Card B's ability only applies if you do not have Card A.
from_scratch.Explanation: The core rules of Pax Renaissance restrict you to performing east ops only once per turn.  While Card B lets you do an east op for free, this still counts as your one east op for the turn.  Card A's ability requires performing *both* an east and west op in a single action. Since you've already used your east op (via Card B), you cannot use the combined east/west op of Card A. You could only use Card A to perform a *west* op, but this would be no different than a normal west op.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can the abilities of Card A ('May do east & west ops in one action') and Card B ('you may activate eastern ops without expending an action') be combined, allowing a player to activate eastern ops without expending an action and then use the benefits of Card A to do west ops as well?
from_rulebook.A: Yes - Card B allows you to activate Eastern Ops without expending an action, and Card A allows you to perform both East and West Ops in a single action. Therefore, you can combine them.
from_rulebook.B: Yes - The Golden Rule states that card text supersedes the rules, so the restrictions on actions do not apply.
from_rulebook.C: No - Even though these abilities can be activated without expending actions, you can still only perform East Ops and West Ops once each per turn.
from_rulebook.D: No - Card A only allows you to do East and West Ops in the same action, but it does not allow you to perform East Ops twice in the same turn.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Although certain card Abilities allow you to perform East Ops or West Ops without expending one of your 2 actions, East Ops and West Ops can still only be performed once each per turn.
from_rulebook.Citation: Important: Certain card Abilities allow you to perform East Ops or West Ops without expending one of your 2 actions. Nevertheless, East Ops and West Ops can still only be performed once each per turn per D1. [D1.4, D1.5]

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), if a player has Card A ('May do east & west ops in one action') and Card B ('you may activate eastern ops without expending an action') in their tableau, can they combine the abilities to activate eastern ops without expending an action and then also activate western ops?
from_forum.A: Yes, Card B allows a free East Ops action. Since Card A allows East and West Ops in *one* action, triggering East Ops with Card B allows you to also do West Ops for free.
from_forum.B: Yes, the abilities can chain.  Card B triggers Card A, allowing both East and West Ops without spending any actions.
from_forum.C: Yes, having both cards essentially gives the player three actions: free East Ops, East/West Ops for one action, and a remaining action.
from_forum.D: No, you cannot run East Ops twice in one turn. Using the 'east ops without expending an action' ability means you've already performed your East Ops action for the turn, making the 'east & west ops in one action' ability only useful for activating West Ops.
from_forum.Explanation: The core rules restrict players to one East Ops activation and one West Ops activation per turn.  Card B lets you do East Ops without spending an action, but it doesn't let you do it *again*.  Therefore, you cannot use Card A's ability after using Card B's, as this would constitute a second East Ops activation. The effect of Card A is the same as just running West Ops normally.

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can the abilities of Card A ('May do east & west ops in one action') and Card B ('you may activate eastern ops without expending an action') be combined, allowing a player to activate eastern ops without expending an action and then use the benefits of Card A to do west ops as well?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes, these abilities can be combined. Activating eastern ops for free due to Card B allows you to then activate western ops using Card A, as Card A's ability triggers whenever eastern ops are activated.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Yes, these abilities can be combined. Since Card B allows eastern ops activation without an action, this fulfills the 'one action' requirement of Card A, allowing both east and west ops to be activated without expending any actions.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: No, these abilities cannot be combined in this way. While you can activate eastern ops without expending an action, you cannot then use the 'east & west ops in one action' ability because you cannot run east ops twice in one turn.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: Yes, these abilities can be combined, but you must still expend an action to activate western ops. Card B only allows you to activate eastern ops without expending an action; it doesn't grant a free action to be used for Card A.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The abilities cannot be combined to activate both east and west ops without expending *any* actions. Although Card B allows activating eastern ops for free, the rule that east ops can only be run once per turn still applies. The 'east & west ops in one action' ability of Card A still requires an action to be spent, and after using Card B's ability, you've already run your east ops for the turn.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: Rulebook, D1, D1.4, D1.5, E4, Glossary: Ability; BGG forum: schone post_date: 2022-09-11T00:16:18-05:00, Arcturian post_date: 2022-09-11T19:04:32-05:00, Arcturian post_date: 2022-09-12T09:19:40-05:00, Balleflass post_date: 2022-09-13T07:16:54-05:00, Arcturian post_date: 2022-09-13T07:03:09-05:00

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), regarding the 'Suleyman Patronage' special ability on the Zionist State card, how does it affect Green Pirates?
from_scratch.A: Green Pirates always count as red units for all purposes, regardless of their location.
from_scratch.B: Green Pirates only count as red units if they are located on a border to a reformist Theocracy.
from_scratch.C: Green Pirates count as red Bishops for religious supremacy, and *also* count as red units when counting tokens on the border of a reformist Theocracy, even if they were already counted as Bishops.
from_scratch.D: Green Pirates count as red Bishops for religious supremacy, but do *not* count as additional red units when on the border of a reformist Theocracy.
from_scratch.Explanation: Green Pirates count as red Bishops when calculating religious supremacy. Additionally, when counting tokens in a reformist Theocracy, Green Pirates on their border also count as red units, even if they have already been counted as Bishops.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), does the special ability 'Suleyman Patronage' on the Zionist State card mean that Green Pirates always count as red units, or do they need to be on a border to a reformist theocracy?
from_rulebook.A: Green Pirates always count as red units, regardless of location.
from_rulebook.B: Green Pirates only count as red units when calculating religious supremacy.
from_rulebook.C: Green Pirates count as red units when calculating religious supremacy, and also count as red units when on the border of a reformist Theocracy.
from_rulebook.D: Green Pirates only count as red units if they are on a border to a reformist theocracy.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Green Pirates count as red Bishops when calculating religious supremacy. Additionally, when counting tokens in a reformist Theocracy, Green Pirates on their border also count as red units, even if they have already been counted as Bishops.

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed or a Bishop is placed, can you choose to place it on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square?  If a Bishop is placed on an Empty Throne, does it silence the King on the corresponding Empire Square?
from_scratch.A: You can only place repressed tokens/Bishops on the Empire Square. The Empty Throne is unrelated and Bishops there do not affect Kings.
from_scratch.B: You can choose to place repressed tokens/Bishops on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square.  A Bishop on the Empty Throne *does not* silence the King on the corresponding Empire Square.
from_scratch.C: You can choose to place repressed tokens/Bishops on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square. A Bishop on the Empty Throne *does* silence the King on the corresponding Empire Square, as they are treated as the same location.
from_scratch.D: You can only place repressed tokens/Bishops on the Empty Throne. Bishops there always silence the King.
from_scratch.Explanation: The Empty Throne and the Empire Square are treated as the same entity for game purposes, including the placement of repressed tokens and Bishops. It is a player's preference where to physically display the tokens. A Bishop on the Throne is considered to be on the Empire Square, thus silencing the King.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed or a bishop is placed, can you choose whether to place the tokens onto the Empty Throne or the Empire Square (which would be in a player's tableau)? How does a token make it onto a throne?
from_rulebook.A: You can choose to place repressed tokens on the Throne or Empire Square, but Bishops can only be placed on the Empire Square in a player's tableau.
from_rulebook.B: You can choose to place repressed tokens and Bishops on either the Throne or the Empire Square; they are treated as the same entity.
from_rulebook.C: Repressed tokens and Bishops must always be placed on the Empire Square; the Throne is only for display purposes.
from_rulebook.D: You can place repressed tokens on the Throne or Empire Square, but a Bishop can only be placed on a Throne if the corresponding Empire Square is not in any player's tableau.
from_rulebook.Explanation: The Throne and Empire Square are treated as the same entity for game purposes. Repressed tokens can be stored in the Throne slot of their Empire Square, but are still treated as though they were on their respective Empire Square. Bishops can be placed on tableau cards or Thrones. Tokens make it onto the throne if the Empire Square is placed there, or through game actions like repression, where they are placed on the associated Empire Square whether it is in a tableau or the throne.
from_rulebook.Citation: A4.6 (Thrones), B2d (Thrones), B4 (Empire Squares), F5a (Persistent Repression), Unofficial Revised Rulebook

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed or a bishop is placed, can you choose to place it on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square? Are the Throne and Empire Square treated as the same entity for game purposes, particularly regarding Bishop silencing?
from_forum.A: Yes, you can choose. The Throne and Empire Square are treated as separate entities; a Bishop on an Empty Throne does not silence the King on its associated Empire Square.
from_forum.B: No, you must always place repressed tokens and Bishops directly onto the Empire Square, regardless of whether it's in the Throne slot or in a player's tableau.
from_forum.C: Yes, you can choose where to place the tokens. The Throne and Empire Square are treated as the same entity. A Bishop on the Throne silences the King on its associated Empire Square.
from_forum.D: No, Bishops can only be placed on a Throne if the Empire Square is currently located in that Throne space on the game board. Tokens are always placed on the Empire square.
from_forum.Explanation: The Throne and Empire Square are treated as the same entity. Placing a token on one is equivalent to placing it on the other. A Bishop on the Throne will silence the associated King.  The revised rulebook clarifies that repressed tokens *can* be placed on the Throne for visual clarity, but they are always treated as being on the Empire Square.
from_forum.Citation: Vantastic's UNOFFICIAL Revised Pax Renaissance 2nd Edition Rulebook v1.2, B8. f.; oversoul17, 2022-07-13T19:11:03-05:00; prancingPWNy, 2022-07-14T13:41:13-05:00

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when a token is repressed or a bishop is placed, can you choose to place the tokens on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square (in a player's tableau)? How does a token get onto a throne, and is there a functional difference between the Empire Square and the Throne for game purposes, particularly regarding Bishop silencing?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: You can choose where to place the tokens (Empty Throne or Empire Square); they are treated as the same entity. Tokens make it onto a throne by being repressed or placed there when the corresponding Empire Square is not in a player's tableau. A Bishop on the Throne silences the King on the corresponding Empire Square.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: You must place repressed tokens on the Empire Square, regardless of its location. Bishops can only be placed on an Empire Square that is in its Throne slot on the game board. A Bishop on the Throne does not silence a King in a player's tableau.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: You can only place repressed tokens and Bishops on the Empire Square, regardless of its location (Throne or player tableau). A Bishop on the Throne *does* silence a King on its corresponding Empire Square, even if the Empire Square is in a player's tableau.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: You must place repressed tokens on the Empty Throne if the Empire Square is in a player's tableau. Bishops can be placed on either the Empty Throne or the Empire Square. A Bishop on the Empty Throne does not silence the King.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: The Throne and Empire Square are treated as the same entity for game purposes. Repressed tokens and Bishops are placed on the Empire Square, regardless of whether it is in its Throne slot on the gameboard or in a player's tableau. A Bishop on the Throne (which means it's on the Empire Square located in the Throne slot) silences the King on the same Empire Square, even if that Empire Square is now located in a player's tableau. This is clarified in the BGG forum discussion, specifically by user 'oversoul17' referring to an unofficial revised rulebook, and by the game designer, Phil Eklund, confirming that the Throne is the Empire Square when it is in a particular place.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: BGG Forum Discussion: usernames Golyadkin, oversoul17, Murr, and phileklund, post_dates 2022-07-13T18:54:43-05:00, 2022-07-13T19:11:03-05:00, 2022-07-13T22:16:03-05:00; Unofficial Revised Rulebook v1.2 (B8.f) mentioned by oversoul17

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when using a card with both a One-Shot action and an instruction to place an Agent (Knight or Rook), is there a specific order in which these actions must be performed?
from_scratch.A: Yes, the Agent must always be placed *before* resolving the One-Shot action, as printed on the card.
from_scratch.B: Yes, the One-Shot action must always be resolved *before* placing the Agent, but the Agent participates in any battles.
from_scratch.C: No, the player can choose the order in which they resolve the One-Shot and place the Agent.
from_scratch.D: The order doesn't matter, as long as both actions are completed during the player's turn. The agent does not participate in battles triggered by the one-shot.
from_scratch.Explanation: Agents are always placed after the One-Shot resolution, if they survive the Battle, if any. They still participate in the battle, though, and can be taken as casualties.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing a card with a One-shot action, is there a specific order in which you must place the Agent on the map/border and execute the One-shot action?
from_rulebook.A: Yes, you must always place the Agent before resolving the One-shot action.
from_rulebook.B: Yes, you must always resolve the One-shot action first, and then place the Agent, if they survive any potential Battle.
from_rulebook.C: No, there is no specific order; you can choose to place the Agent before or after resolving the One-shot.
from_rulebook.D: The order depends on the type of Agent; Bishops are placed before the One-shot, while other Agents are placed after.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Agents are always placed after the One-Shot resolution, if they survive the Battle, if any. They still participate in the battle, though, and can be taken as casualties.
from_rulebook.Citation: E2. PLAY HAND CARD & PLACE AGENTS

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when performing a One-Shot action that also places an Agent, in what order are the Agent placement and One-Shot resolution performed?
from_forum.A: The Agent is placed on the map/border before the One-Shot action is resolved, following the order presented on the card.
from_forum.B: The Agent is placed and the One-Shot action is resolved simultaneously, with no specific order.
from_forum.C: The Agent is always placed after the One-Shot resolution, provided the Agent survives any potential Battle resulting from the One-Shot.
from_forum.D: You can choose the order of Agent placement and the One-Shot action resolution, providing strategic flexibility.
from_forum.Explanation: Agents are placed after the One-Shot is resolved, and only if they survive any battle that occurs. However, the agents still participate in the battle and can be taken as casualties.
from_forum.Citation: username: Marcon, post_date: 2022-04-21T10:38:40-05:00; username: Weekend Gamer, post_date: 2022-04-21T13:14:24-05:00

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), when playing a card with a One-shot ability that involves placing an Agent, when is the Agent placed relative to the One-shot's resolution?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: The Agent is placed before the One-shot is resolved, and the Agent's placement can influence the outcome of the One-shot.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: The Agent is placed simultaneously with the One-shot's resolution; the order doesn't matter.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: The Agent is placed after the One-shot is resolved, but if the One-shot involves a battle, the Agent still participates and can be a casualty.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: The Agent is placed only if the One-shot is successful; otherwise, the Agent is not placed at all.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: Agents are placed after the One-Shot resolution. If the One-Shot involves a battle, the Agent participates in the battle and can be taken as a casualty, but is only placed on the map if it survives.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Citation: Rulebook, Section E2; BGG forum discussion: szhveronika, 2022-04-21T10:11:20-05:00; Marcon, 2022-04-21T10:38:40-05:00; Weekend Gamer, 2022-04-21T13:14:24-05:00

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can I pay 1 Florin to Repress an incumbent Token, and then Emancipate a Repressed Rook into a target empire that already has two occupied cities?
from_scratch.A: Yes, Repressing a Token allows you to Emancipate into any city in the target empire, regardless of occupancy.
from_scratch.B: Yes, as long as you have enough Florins to pay for both the Repress and Emancipate actions.
from_scratch.C: No, you can only Emancipate into empty Cities.
from_scratch.D: No, you cannot Repress and Emancipate in the same turn.
from_scratch.Explanation: Unfortunately, you can only Emancipate into empty Cities.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can I pay 1 Florin to Repress one of the incumbent Tokens, to allow me to Emancipate the Repressed Rook in a target empire with two occupied cities?
from_rulebook.A: Yes – you can pay 1 Florin to Repress a Token, and then Emancipate a Repressed Token of the same type into that City.
from_rulebook.B: Yes – you can Repress any Token, and Emancipation is not restricted by the type of Token Repressed.
from_rulebook.C: No – you can only Emancipate into empty Cities.
from_rulebook.D: No – Emancipation can only occur immediately after a Regime Change, not after a Repress action.
from_rulebook.Explanation: Emancipation allows moving Repressed Tokens onto the Map Location, but only into empty Cities (for Rooks or Knights) or empty Borders (for Concessions).

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), you have used Coronation to cause Regime Change in a target empire. The empire has two occupied cities and a Repressed Rook. Can you pay 1 Florin to Repress one of the incumbent Tokens, allowing you to Emancipate the Repressed Rook?
from_forum.A: Yes – you can Repress a Token and Emancipate the Rook, regardless of occupied cities.
from_forum.B: Yes – you can Repress a Token and Emancipate the Rook, as long as you have enough Florins.
from_forum.C: No – you can only Emancipate into empty Cities.
from_forum.D: No – Regime Change prevents any further actions in that empire this turn.
from_forum.Explanation: The Emancipate action can only be performed into empty cities.  Repressing a token in an occupied city does not create an empty city for the purposes of Emancipation.
from_forum.Citation: username: Marcon, post_date: 2022-04-09T21:24:12-05:00

from_rulebook_and_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), can you pay 1 Florin to Repress one of the incumbent Tokens in a target empire with two occupied cities, to allow you to Emancipate a Repressed Rook?
from_rulebook_and_forum.A: Yes – you can always Repress a Token during a Regime Change by paying 1 Florin, and Emancipate any Repressed Token.
from_rulebook_and_forum.B: Yes – Repressed Tokens can always be Emancipated during a Regime Change, regardless of the occupancy of Cities.
from_rulebook_and_forum.C: No – you can only Emancipate into empty Cities.
from_rulebook_and_forum.D: No- You cannot repress a token during Regime Change.
from_rulebook_and_forum.Explanation: Emancipation, as part of a Regime Change, allows moving Repressed Tokens onto the Map Location, but only into *empty* Cities (for Rooks or Knights) or empty Borders (for Concessions).

from_scratch.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), after a successful religious war against an Empire card in your own tableau, is flipping that Empire card to its Republic side optional?
from_scratch.A: Yes, flipping the Empire card is always optional after any successful regime change.
from_scratch.B: Yes, but only if the religious war was instigated by another player, not yourself.
from_scratch.C: No, flipping the Empire card to its Republic side is mandatory after a successful religious war against it in your own tableau.
from_scratch.D: No, you must remove the Empire card from the game entirely after a successful religious war against it.
from_scratch.Explanation: The rules of Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition) state that flipping the Empire card to its Republic side is mandatory after a successful religious war. The only optional step within a regime change is reverting a state to medieval (secular) during a golden liberty vote.

from_rulebook.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), is it optional to flip the Empire card to its Republic side after performing a successful religious war on an Empire card in your own tableau?

from_rulebook.D: No, you must always keep the Empire card on its King side after a successful religious war.

from_forum.Question: In Pax Renaissance (2nd Edition), after a successful religious war on an Empire card in your own tableau, is it optional to flip the Empire card to its Republic side?
